caveats: a: The OEWS table is from May 2023 and covers employers of all sizes, not only the LMM lens.
a: Occupation categories do not reveal what portion of each job is already automated or newly AI-exposed.
a: Sales representatives often perform physical inspections, and managers may perform field supervision, reducing exposure relative to their occupational titles.
rho: Survey belief, research, evaluation, or budget allocation is not operational implementation.
rho: The underlying respondent frames differ and are not limited to US LMM roofing contractors.
rho: Accuracy failures in measurements, scopes, contracts, and claims can erase labor savings or create liability.
e: The supplied LMM firm count is margin-bridged and estimated rather than directly observed.
e: Residential, storm-restoration, and commercial roofing have different transferability and revenue quality.
e: The broad lens includes repeat project revenue even when formal recurring contracts are absent.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than roofing contractors or the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: Survey plans may not become completed transactions.
s5: Closures, minority investments, internal reorganizations, and nontransferable owner exits are excluded.
q: No roofing-specific study was found measuring pass-through of AI-enabled labor savings.
q: Insurance restoration, retail replacement, commercial bids, and maintenance contracts have materially different price transparency.
q: Demand effects and implementation costs are intentionally excluded from this primitive.
d5: Occupational employment is not constant-price, constant-quality roofing service demand.
d5: The BLS projection is national and ten-year rather than five-year.
d5: The reroofing survey is US and Canada, self-selected, and reports one year-over-year quarter rather than a structural forecast.
d5: The supplied compensation ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.

o: Simple inspections and measurements can shift away from contractors without eliminating installation accountability.
o: Residential and commercial roof systems differ in amenability to standardization and automation.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue comes from fixed-price bids, negotiated commercial contracts, unit-priced work, insurance-funded residential restoration, service and maintenance agreements, and time-and-material repairs for homeowners, property managers, general contractors, institutions, and commercial building owners.
lens.excluded_activities: Roofing-material manufacturing or distribution without contracting, captive owner or general-contractor crews, solar installation without roofing responsibility, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and businesses lacking a transferable operating organization.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors installing, replacing, repairing, coating, inspecting, and maintaining residential, commercial, and institutional roof systems for external customers, including firms whose recurring activity is a sequence of projects, storm-restoration jobs, maintenance calls, and reroofing work.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages by occupation inside NAICS 238160, not task-level AI exposure. I use the observed mix—69.04% construction/extraction employment versus 11.20% office/administrative, 6.94% management, 5.52% business/financial, and 4.01% sales—and apply higher exposure judgment to document, estimating, sales-support, scheduling, and administrative tasks and low exposure to physical field tasks. Wage weighting raises the exposed share relative to a headcount calculation because management, business, and sales wages exceed trade wages; task overlap and partial exposure create error in both directions.
caveats: The OEWS table is from May 2023 and covers employers of all sizes, not only the LMM lens.
Occupation categories do not reveal what portion of each job is already automated or newly AI-exposed.
Sales representatives often perform physical inspections, and managers may perform field supervision, reducing exposure relative to their occupational titles.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries from a representative sample of NAICS 238160 firms, paired with controlled measurements of labor hours avoided by AI-enabled estimating, claims, CRM, dispatch, and accounting tools, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Core roofing work is physical, site-variable, weather-exposed, and safety-critical. The credible AI opportunity lies in aerial-image and drawing interpretation, takeoff and estimating, lead qualification, proposal and claim-document drafting, procurement, crew scheduling, job documentation, invoicing, collections, and customer communications. Human judgment remains necessary for roof inspection, scope validation, exceptions, and field execution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects roofer employment to rise 6% from 166,700 in 2024 to 176,500 in 2034 and attributes demand to replacement, repair, new roofs, and some rooftop solar. Converting the ten-year employment projection to roughly five years suggests about 3% growth, used as the base quantity ratio. Employment may understate demand if productivity rises and overstate it if labor scarcity restricts supply; reroofing survey dispersion supports a wider cyclical range.
caveats: Occupational employment is not constant-price, constant-quality roofing service demand.
The BLS projection is national and ten-year rather than five-year.
The reroofing survey is US and Canada, self-selected, and reports one year-over-year quarter rather than a structural forecast.
The supplied compensation ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast combining constant-price roofing revenue, roof-stock age and material life, claims-frequency data, reroofing squares, maintenance volumes, construction put in place, and regional storm-normalized demand would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The installed roof stock creates nondiscretionary repair and eventual replacement demand, while new construction and rooftop solar add work. Weather, insurance conditions, interest rates, and construction cycles create substantial year-to-year volatility, but they do not remove the underlying need to keep buildings watertight.

caveats: The supplied LMM firm count is margin-bridged and estimated rather than directly observed.
Residential, storm-restoration, and commercial roofing have different transferability and revenue quality.
The broad lens includes repeat project revenue even when formal recurring contracts are absent.
e.change_evidence: A census of the 216 estimated LMM firms using contractor-license data, owner dependence, crew employment model, safety history, customer and insurer concentration, maintenance revenue, storm normalization, and sale-readiness interviews would materially narrow the range.
e.rationale: Most firms at the supplied $1-10M EBITDA scale should have transferable crews, supervisors, equipment, supplier and customer relationships, licenses, systems, and a repeat outsourced-service model. Eligibility is reduced by owner-centric selling, customer or storm-event concentration, dependence on transient subcontract crews, weak safety or claims practices, geographic licensing constraints, and businesses whose earnings normalize poorly outside unusually severe weather years.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23816 ($17,882,873,561) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23816 ($68,783,457,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23816. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $335,532 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238160); class <5: 15,714 firms, avg revenue/firm $621,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $49,453 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,792 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,164,947 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $172,330 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,328 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,265,212 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $339,511 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,976 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,544,742 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $998,561 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 216 firms, avg revenue/firm $51,879,940 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,129,643 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $271,528,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $21,613,629 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 216

caveats: The range is judgmental and future robotics adoption is uncertain.
Simple inspections and measurements can shift away from contractors without eliminating installation accountability.
Residential and commercial roof systems differ in amenability to standardization and automation.
o.change_evidence: Verified production data from robotic shingle installation, automated roof inspection and repair, modular roofing systems, insurer-directed self-service claims, and general contractors or property owners self-performing roofing would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five roofing demand still requires an accountable contractor to inspect conditions, select and procure systems, coordinate access and safety, remove or prepare surfaces, install and waterproof materials, manage weather exceptions, and warrant workmanship. Software and drones can reduce inspection, measurement, and office effort; limited robotics or prefabrication may reduce field hours, but they do not plausibly eliminate the responsible operator across the current service basket within five years.

caveats: No roofing-specific study was found measuring pass-through of AI-enabled labor savings.
Insurance restoration, retail replacement, commercial bids, and maintenance contracts have materially different price transparency.
Demand effects and implementation costs are intentionally excluded from this primitive.
q.change_evidence: Job-level gross-margin bridges by contract type, insurer versus retail payment, renewal or rebid pricing, competitive win-loss records, and customer interviews about expected productivity givebacks would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-price residential and commercial jobs allow an operator to retain labor savings on work already contracted, while faster response and greater estimating capacity can support share gains without immediate price cuts. Over five years, competitive bids, visible insurance scopes, negotiated commercial customers, software diffusion, and renewal repricing should pass a substantial share of the benefit through; time-and-material work shares it faster.

rho.bridge: Two surveys cover construction professionals and general/trade contractors rather than LMM roofing firms or realized labor substitution. DEWALT reported 8% current on-job AI use, 37% piloting or researching, and 90% expecting AI to be indispensable within five years; Dodge reported 51% evaluating AI changes and 40% allocating an AI budget. I translate this adoption pipeline into implementation of about half the exposed roofing opportunity within five years. Broad surveys may overstate readiness of small roofers but understate roofing's fit with image-based measurement, estimating, and lead workflows.
caveats: Survey belief, research, evaluation, or budget allocation is not operational implementation.
The underlying respondent frames differ and are not limited to US LMM roofing contractors.
Accuracy failures in measurements, scopes, contracts, and claims can erase labor savings or create liability.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal NRCA survey of roofing firms by revenue size that reports production deployment, workflow coverage, error rates, training cost, and verified labor hours avoided would replace the broad-construction adoption proxies.
rho.rationale: Commercial tools can enter through estimating, CRM, accounting, inspection imagery, and documentation systems, and severe hiring difficulty creates incentive. Implementation is limited by training, fragmented job data, integration cost, output accuracy, privacy, insurance documentation standards, field adoption, and the need for human validation.

s5.bridge: Gallup observed that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I discount that broad-employer intent rate because gifts, rare public offerings, internal family transitions, incomplete plans, and non-control deals are not all qualifying control transfers. Roofing trade demographics and consolidation interest could lift actual sale activity, while owner dependence and volatile earnings could reduce completion.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than roofing contractors or the LMM EBITDA band.
Survey plans may not become completed transactions.
Closures, minority investments, internal reorganizations, and nontransferable owner exits are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Closed roofing-transaction data from GF Data, Axial, CapIQ, BizBuySell, broker CRM systems, and SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, divided by an identified eligible-firm population, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer firms have substantially more five-year transfer intent than nonemployers, but completed qualifying control sales are a subset of intent. The estimated range therefore remains below the observed broad-employer plan rate.

principal_driver: A measurable office, estimating, business, and sales layer can use AI to increase quote and job throughput amid persistent construction hiring difficulty.
principal_weakness: Most industry employment is hands-on construction, limiting wage-weighted substitution and leaving realized savings dependent on disciplined workflow integration.
